name: Strontium
name_origin: Named after the Scottish village of Strontian, where the mineral strontianite was first discovered in 1790.

discovery.discoverer: Adair Crawford and William Cruickshank (identified); isolated by Humphry Davy in 1808

occurrence.abundance_summary: Strontium is the 15th most abundant element in Earth's crust at about 360 ppm. It occurs primarily in the minerals celestite (SrSO4) and strontianite (SrCO3), and is also present in seawater at about 8 ppm.
states: trace in plant and animal tissues

compounds.name: Strontium carbonate
compounds.summary: Naturally occurring mineral strontianite, used in ceramics, pyrotechnics, and cathode-ray tube glass.

compounds.name: Strontium sulfate
compounds.summary: The mineral celestite, the principal commercial source of strontium.

compounds.name: Strontium nitrate
compounds.summary: Used in pyrotechnics and signal flares to produce a brilliant red flame.

compounds.name: Strontium chloride
compounds.summary: Used in toothpaste for sensitive teeth and as a precursor in strontium chemistry.

compounds.name: Strontium oxide
compounds.summary: An alkaline earth oxide used in specialty optical glasses and ceramics.

uses: Pyrotechnics and red-color flares (strontium nitrate and strontium carbonate)
Ferrite ceramic magnets (strontium ferrite) for loudspeakers and small motors
Cathode-ray tube glass to block X-rays (historic use)
Strontium ranelate as a treatment for osteoporosis (medical use)
reactivity_summary: Strontium is a soft, silvery-white alkaline earth metal that quickly turns yellow on exposure to air due to oxide formation. It reacts vigorously with water to liberate hydrogen and form strontium hydroxide, and burns in air with a brilliant crimson flame. Finely divided strontium can ignite spontaneously, so it is typically stored under mineral oil.
isotopes_summary: Strontium has four stable naturally occurring isotopes: 84Sr (~0.56%), 86Sr (~9.86%), 87Sr (~7.00%), and 88Sr (~82.58%). The radioactive 90Sr (half-life 28.8 years) is a notable fission product that biologically substitutes for calcium and accumulates in bone; it is used in medical and industrial applications but poses environmental concerns.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
